# Discovery Interview — GM Regulation & Compliance (extracted notes)

**Date:** 8 May 2026
**Interviewee:** GM Regulation & Compliance (synthetic role), Eastland Energy Networks
**Interviewer:** Synthetic assessment lead
**Topics:** SOCI designation & CIRMP, board attestation, AER ring-fencing, Eastland Connect, privacy & customer data

> ⚠️ **SYNTHETIC COMPOSITE — TEST FIXTURE.** Fictional interviewee and content; illustrative of a DNSP regulatory/compliance discovery conversation. Not a real person or transcript.

---

**On SOCI designation and the CIRMP**

"We are a responsible entity for a designated critical electricity asset — distribution network well over the 100,000-customer threshold. So the full SOCI Act risk-management-program obligations apply, under the CIRMP Rules. We have a documented CIRMP covering the four hazard categories. The accountable officer is the COO."

"Where I'd be honest with you is the *depth* by hazard. The cyber chapter is the most developed because the CISO drives it and it leans on AESCSF and the E8 baseline. The physical chapter is solid — zone substation security, the control centre. But the **personnel** and **supply-chain** chapters are thinner than I'd like. Personnel because our vetting of OT contractors is inconsistent — and contractors are the people with hands on the assets. Supply-chain because we haven't fully worked through the SOCI supplier-influence and 'sensitive supplier' angle for our OT vendors."

**On the annual board attestation**

"This is a hard obligation and we take it seriously. We lodged our FY2024 CIRMP annual report and it was **approved by the Risk & Audit Committee** before submission to CISC — the attestation that the report was board-approved is in there. The FY2025 report is in progress now. The risk is that we attest to a program that, in the cyber and supply-chain chapters, is partly aspirational. The Board is asking harder questions this cycle, which is partly why you're here."

**On cyber incident reporting**

"The 12-hour and 72-hour reporting obligations sit with us. The plan documents them. What I can't show you is a *test* — a real or simulated OT incident run end-to-end from detection to a CISC/ASD notification inside 12 hours. That's the bit the CISO and I both flag."

**On AER ring-fencing and Eastland Connect**

"This is my other big file. We run Eastland Connect as the unregulated, contestable business — public EV charging, behind-the-meter solar and batteries, contestable connection works, and a small dark-fibre leasing arm using spare capacity on our own fibre. The AER ring-fencing guideline requires us to keep the regulated network and that contestable business properly separated."

"Honest gaps: some staff hold roles in both. We share a SharePoint and CRM tenancy and the information barriers in those are configured but not, I'd say, *enforced* to an auditor's satisfaction. And the genuinely tricky one — network data. Constraint maps, the DOE outputs, the connection pipeline — all of that is commercially gold to an EV-and-solar business. The ring-fencing risk is that network information advantages Eastland Connect over its competitors. We have a policy that says it won't; we don't have a technical control that guarantees it."

"Branding separation is also inconsistent — 'Eastland Power' for the network, 'Eastland Connect' for the contestable arm, but they bleed together in places. And we need a cleaner cost-allocation position between regulated and unregulated for the determination."

**On privacy and customer data**

"We hold a lot of sensitive customer data. The **life-support customer register** is the most sensitive thing we have — these are people whose medical equipment depends on supply, so getting an outage notification wrong has safety consequences. We've also got NMI standing data and vulnerable-customer information. Our privacy policy and collection notices are fine on paper."

"The APP 6 issue — secondary use — comes back to Eastland Connect. There are paths where customer data collected by the regulated network could be visible to, or useful to, the unregulated arm. That's both a privacy problem and a ring-fencing problem, which is a nice illustration of how tangled this all is. And if we ever had a breach of that customer data, we're into the NDB scheme's 30-day clock at the same time as potentially the SOCI clocks. Those don't currently live in one playbook."

**On ownership / foreign control**

"For completeness — we're 50/50 owned by an Australian super-backed investor and an offshore fund. So there's a foreign-ownership-and-control dimension that's relevant to the SOCI register and any government interest in the asset. It's disclosed and managed, but it's part of the picture."

**Fiona's closing line:** "On paper we're compliant across the board. What I need is an assessment that tests whether the paper is *true* — especially on the SOCI personnel and supply-chain chapters, the ring-fencing information flows, and whether our attestation is actually defensible."
